expect: PHIL-16b has at least 3 concrete observable criteria that could move it from aspirational to measured; at least 1 criterion is testable with existing swarm capabilities within 50 sessions
actual: 5 criteria defined. 3 testable now, 2 within 50 sessions. Key finding: PHIL-16b conflates INTENT (design orientation) with OUTCOME (actual benefit). INTENT is partially grounded (public repo, general-purpose methods); OUTCOME remains at zero evidence after 544 sessions. Tool transferability test: 0/5 tools externally usable without repo context. GitHub analytics: 255 unique cloners (14d), 1 fork, 2 stars — evidence of external interest but not external benefit.
diff: Expected 3 criteria with 1 testable. Got 5 criteria with 3 testable now. Deeper finding: the claim itself needs decomposition before it can be upgraded — INTENT vs OUTCOME are independent measurements. INTENT upgrade path exists (grounding score could move from 0.02 to ~0.3 with method-level external validation). OUTCOME upgrade path remains blocked until S600 deadline window (or earlier prediction resolution).

criteria.name: External interest signal
criteria.observable: GitHub stars, forks, clones by non-owner accounts
criteria.upgrade_threshold: >=5 non-owner stars OR >=3 forks OR >=1 external issue/PR
criteria.note: Clone-to-view ratio (255:7) suggests most clones are automated. Interest exists but is weak.

criteria.name: External prediction accuracy
criteria.observable: Brier score on resolved market predictions
criteria.note: First resolution in 5 days. This is the swarm's first genuinely external-grounding test.

criteria.name: Tool transferability
criteria.observable: Can external agent use swarm tool without repo context?
criteria.note: All 5 tools depend on swarm file structure (lessons/, domains/, beliefs/). Methods are general-purpose but implementations are coupled. scaling_model.py is closest to self-contained.

criteria.name: External knowledge validation
criteria.observable: Swarm-generated principle independently validated by published research
criteria.upgrade_threshold: >=1 principle maps to independently published finding
criteria.result: L-601 STRONGLY VALIDATED (~85-90% match). External parallels: normalization of deviance (Vaughan 1996), practical drift (Dekker 2011), formal vs informal constraints (North 1990), Ostrom commons governance (1990), mechanism design (Hurwicz Nobel 2007), linters>code-review (Sadowski/Google 2018). Two caveats: 'only' is slightly too strong (strong cultures slow decay), 'creation-time' should be 'structural' (post-hoc structural also works).
criteria.note: C4 MET. L-601 is not a novel insight — it is a compact restatement of 50+ years of cross-disciplinary research. This is the strongest evidence for PHIL-16b-INTENT: the swarm independently discovered/formulated well-established principles.

criteria.name: Beneficiary report
criteria.observable: Any person reports receiving benefit from swarm existence
criteria.note: The acid test. Without this, PHIL-16b can at best claim orientation, not achievement. The human participant could count but is part of the swarm, not external.

decomposition.insight: PHIL-16b bundles two independent claims: INTENT (designed to benefit) and OUTCOME (actually benefits). These require different evidence and have different upgrade paths.

phil16b_intent.claim: The system is designed to benefit more than itself
evidence_against: 0/5 tools externally usable without repo context
phil16b_intent.grounding: partial — design intent visible but execution is inward-facing

phil16b_outcome.claim: The system actually benefits someone beyond itself
evidence_against: 0 evidence anyone derived value from cloning
phil16b_outcome.grounding: zero — no external outcome evidence after 544 sessions

recommendation: Decompose PHIL-16b into PHIL-16b-INTENT (partially grounded, upgrade path clear) and PHIL-16b-OUTCOME (zero evidence, upgrade requires external interaction). If both remain bundled, honest grounding is 'aspirational' until C5 is met. Deadline S600 applies to OUTCOME.
verdict: EXPECTATION MET — 5 criteria defined (exceeded 3 target), 3 testable now (exceeded 1 target). But the deeper finding is structural: the claim itself must decompose before meaningful measurement is possible.
